next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The call came to Dominic's desk phone a little after three, while Nate was pretending to understand a stack of contractor revisions for a retail pad in Milford. The fluorescent lights in the Kassos office made everything look tired before it was tired. Carpet tile, humming desktops, coffee that tasted like the inside of a wet cardboard box. Ray was at the copier feeding in a packet one page at a time because the machine kept chewing the corners.

Nate almost let the phone ring out. Then he saw Ray glance over and picked it up.

"Dom speaking."

"Jesus Christ, there you are." The voice was male, fast, cheerful in a way that felt practiced. "It's Marco. Marco DeSantis. I had to get your number from your mother because apparently you vanished from the surface of the earth."

Marco meant nothing to him for half a second, then everything at once. Real life. One more person Dominic had accumulated before Nate arrived and forgot to leave instructions for.

Nate said, "Hey."

"Hey?" Marco laughed. "That's what I get? I'm in Bridgeport for one night and I get hey?"

There was a pause in which Nate could either confess ignorance to a stranger with access to Dominic's mother or agree to see him. He could feel the office around him, the slow machinery of attention. Ray lifting pages from the copier. Someone in the break room laughing too hard at something Victor had probably said.

"Sorry," Nate said. "Long day."

"Yeah? Mine too. My mother had cataract surgery this morning, I'm running on hospital coffee, and if I go back to the Marriott and watch cable by myself I'm goi...
open_hooks_to_carry: Nate's preparation: studying files, rehearsing arguments, the false confidence of someone who has crammed
The meeting with Victor: Nate's surface-level arguments dismantled one by one
prev_tail_excerpt:
...with the coffee in one hand, shoulders set a little forward from habit, not age. Mort let himself into the store with his elbow because both hands were full, and the door swung wide enough for Nate to get one more brief rectangle of the place inside: ladder endcaps, fluorescent light, the dark line of the counter. Then the door closed.

Nate got to his car before the shaking started in earnest.

He sat behind the wheel with both hands locked around it, Dominic's broad fingers whitening over the textured plastic, the scar across the left knuckle gone pale. The cup of diner coffee in the holder shivered against its lid. He'd missed the key slot on the first try and left the key hanging there crooked. Across the lot, life went on with its obscene administrative calm. Somebody came out of the nail salon laughing. Gail wiped a table. A delivery van idled at the curb. Mort was back in the store Nate had spent half his life remembering only at the moment of its destruction.

Nobody asked us.

Guy named Altieri signed the letter.

He put his forehead briefly against the top of the steering wheel and smelled vinyl, stale sun, the faint ghost of Al's talc from the haircut, deli turkey on his hands, coffee cooling beside him. He had bought his father a coffee. That was the whole transaction. A stranger named Dom had bought Mort Lazar a coffee and watched him carry it back into the building that was going to be taken from him.

That smallness hurt worse than any dramatic version would've.

By the time Nate started the car his hands were still shaking, only now they had somewhere to go.
state_deltas_required: Nate's first attempt to save the strip mall from inside collides with his professional incompetence and Victor's institutional power
Nate's internal sabotage strategy fails publicly, exposing his incompetence to Victor and straining Ray's protective loyalty. The margin for error in the impersonation shrinks.
